codeReview.summary: The soft_limits.rs implementation fully satisfies the feature requirements: (1) Warning alerts at configurable thresholds for task count, time, cost, and failure count; (2) No-progress detection with both iteration-count and time-based detection; (3) All thresholds and timeouts are configurable via SoftLimitsConfig. The implementation includes 32 unit tests, all passing.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The feature has no worker session (workerSessionIds is empty in features.json) and no handoffs/transcripts exist in the mission directory. This suggests the feature was either committed directly or the session tracking is incomplete. The soft_limits.rs was added in commit 1150939 ('feat(orchestrator): add session hygiene with 60-minute checkpoint interval and progress evaluation') but the current git status shows no uncommitted changes to soft_limits.rs - only merge_queue formatting changes.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: features.json shows workerSessionIds: [], handoffs/ directory does not exist, worker-transcripts.jsonl does not exist

sharedStateObservations.observation: The feature's skillName is 'Rust' but there's no evidence of skill usage in a worker transcript. The implementation follows idiomatic Rust patterns (Arc<RwLock> for shared state, async_trait where needed, thiserror patterns, tracing for logging) as documented in the AGENTS.md coding conventions.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: soft_limits.rs uses Arc<RwLock<SoftLimits>>, tokio::sync::RwLock, tracing::info for logging - all consistent with AGENTS.md conventions

summary: PASS - The orch-soft-limits feature is correctly implemented with all 32 tests passing. The implementation provides: (1) Warning alerts at configurable percentage thresholds for task count (80%), time (80%), cost (80%), and failure count (70%); (2) No-progress detection based on both iterations without progress (warning at 3, critical at 5) and timeout without progress (default 300 seconds); (3) Full configurability via SoftLimitsConfig struct with serde serialization support. The only observation is that the feature lacks a worker session record, suggesting it may have been committed outside the normal scrutiny workflow.
